stem: Your CI pipeline runs Claude Code to review pull requests for inaccurate code comments. The prompt instructs: "Check that comments are accurate. Be conservative and only report high-confidence findings." Despite this, 40% of comment findings are false positives: docstrings flagged for stylistic wording, TODO comments flagged as inaccurate, and incomplete-but-correct comments flagged as wrong. Developers are losing trust in the review. What prompt change would most effectively improve precision?
correct_choice: Replace the vague instruction with explicit categorical criteria, e.g., "Flag a comment only when its claimed behavior contradicts the actual code behavior. Do not flag style issues, TODO markers, or comments that are incomplete but not wrong."
correct_rationale: Explicit categorical criteria define the decision boundary the model should apply: what to flag versus what to skip. General instructions like "be conservative" or "high-confidence only" fail to improve precision because they don't tell the model which findings are wanted — the exam guide calls this out directly ("flag comments only when claimed behavior contradicts actual code behavior" vs "check that comments are accurate").
near_miss_choice: Add the instruction "Only report findings where you are at least 90% confident the comment is inaccurate" so low-confidence findings are suppressed.
near_miss_rationale: Numeric self-assessed confidence looks rigorous but is still a vague meta-instruction: LLM self-reported confidence is poorly calibrated, and the model is already "confident" in the false positives it emits. It never defines which categories of finding are in or out of scope.
distractor1_choice: Add few-shot examples demonstrating the required output format for findings (location, issue, severity, suggested fix).
distractor1_rationale: Format-focused few-shot examples improve output consistency, not decision precision. The failure here is which comments get flagged, not how findings are formatted — examples would need to demonstrate the accept/reject boundary, and the more direct first step is stating the explicit criteria.
distractor2_choice: Run the review twice and only report findings that appear in both runs.
distractor2_rationale: Consensus filtering suppresses real findings that are caught intermittently and doubles cost, while systematic false positives (the model consistently flagging TODOs) survive both runs. It treats the symptom without fixing the unclear criteria that cause misclassification.
anti_pattern: Controlling review precision with vague meta-instructions ("be conservative", "only high-confidence findings") instead of explicit categorical criteria that define exactly which issues to report and which to skip. The model cannot infer the intended decision boundary from an adverb.

stem: Your automated PR review reports findings in five categories. Measured precision: security 92%, correctness 89%, style 88%, documentation 85% — but the performance category runs at a 55% false-positive rate. Developers have started dismissing all review output without reading it, including accurate security findings. You need to restore trust while you improve the performance prompts. What should you do first?
correct_choice: Temporarily disable the performance category, keep the high-precision categories running, and re-enable performance only after refining its criteria with concrete code examples per severity level and measuring improved precision.
correct_rationale: High false-positive categories undermine confidence in accurate categories — developer trust is a shared resource across the whole review. Temporarily disabling the offending category stops the trust damage immediately while the prompt is improved offline; explicit severity criteria with concrete code examples give the category a measurable path back.
near_miss_choice: Keep the performance category enabled but downgrade all its findings to informational severity so developers can ignore them more easily.
near_miss_rationale: Downgrading severity still delivers the noise — developers still see wrong findings attributed to the tool, and trust continues to erode. It also buries any genuine performance issues at a severity nobody reads, giving the worst of both worlds.
distractor1_choice: Add "be more careful and conservative with performance findings" to the review prompt and monitor whether precision improves.
distractor1_rationale: Vague carefulness instructions do not improve precision — the model lacks explicit criteria for what constitutes a real performance issue. Meanwhile the category keeps emitting 55% false positives in production, continuing to poison trust in the accurate categories.
distractor2_choice: Require developers to dismiss each performance finding with a written reason so you can collect data while keeping the category live.
distractor2_rationale: This shifts the cost of the tool's imprecision onto developers, accelerating the very disengagement you're trying to reverse. Dismissal-reason data is useful, but collecting it by forcing triage of known-bad output sacrifices trust for telemetry.
anti_pattern: Leaving a known high-false-positive finding category live while iterating on it. High-FP categories undermine developer confidence in the accurate categories, so the whole review gets ignored.

stem: Your support resolution agent handles returns with a detailed policy prompt: refunds within 30 days, replacements for verified damage, escalation for policy exceptions. On clear-cut cases it performs well. On ambiguous cases — a damage report on day 35 where shipping took 12 days, a return request for an item bought as a gift before a policy change — its decisions are inconsistent across runs, sometimes refunding, sometimes refusing the same fact pattern. The policy text is already thorough. What's the most effective improvement?
correct_choice: Add 2-4 few-shot examples of ambiguous cases, each showing the decision and the reasoning for why that action was chosen over the plausible alternative.
correct_rationale: Detailed instructions alone produce inconsistent results on ambiguous inputs; targeted few-shot examples that demonstrate the reasoning behind a judgment call teach the model the decision boundary and let it generalize that judgment to novel ambiguous patterns — exactly the ambiguous-case role the exam guide assigns to few-shot prompting.
near_miss_choice: Extend the policy prompt with explicit if-then rules covering every ambiguous situation observed so far (late damage with slow shipping, gift purchases across policy changes, etc.).
near_miss_rationale: Enumerated rules only cover the pre-specified cases — the next novel ambiguity is again undefined, and the rule list grows without bound. Few-shot examples showing reasoning generalize to patterns you didn't anticipate; exhaustive rule enumeration does not.
distractor1_choice: Add the instruction "When a case is ambiguous, use your best judgment and apply the policy consistently across similar cases."
distractor1_rationale: The model has no memory of how it decided "similar cases" in other conversations, and "best judgment" is exactly what's producing inconsistency. Vague consistency exhortations give the model no new information about where the decision boundary lies.
distractor2_choice: Route every ambiguous case to a human agent so the model only ever decides clear-cut cases.
distractor2_rationale: Escalation is appropriate for genuine policy gaps, but routing all ambiguity to humans abandons a large share of resolvable volume and your first-contact resolution target. These cases are decidable once the boundary is demonstrated — teaching it with examples is the proportionate fix.

stem: You extract structured metadata (authors, sample size, methodology) from research papers. Papers vary widely: some cite inline, others use numbered bibliographies; some report sample sizes in tables, others in narrative prose like "we recruited roughly two hundred participants". Your prompt contains three paragraphs of prose rules describing how to handle each style, yet the model still misattributes bibliography entries as authors and fabricates a numeric sample_size when none is stated. What change is most likely to fix both behaviors?
correct_choice: Add few-shot examples demonstrating correct extraction from each document structure — including one example whose source lacks a sample size and whose output correctly returns null for that field.
correct_rationale: When prose descriptions are interpreted inconsistently, concrete input/output examples are the most effective way to communicate the expected transformation. Examples across varied structures (inline citations vs bibliographies, tables vs narrative) teach structure-specific reading, and an explicit absent-field example demonstrates returning null instead of fabricating — the exam guide names both as few-shot roles in extraction.
near_miss_choice: Rewrite the prose rules to be more precise, adding a dedicated subsection for every document format you've encountered.
near_miss_rationale: The prompt already fails through prose — more prose keeps the same failure mode. Detailed natural-language descriptions of transformations are interpreted inconsistently; demonstrations communicate the mapping directly and generalize to formats the rules didn't anticipate.
distractor1_choice: Run each extraction three times and take the majority value for each field.
distractor1_rationale: Self-consistency voting triples cost and doesn't help when the error is systematic: a model that reliably misreads bibliographies or fabricates a plausible sample size will produce the same wrong answer in all three runs. Voting addresses random variance, not learned misreading.
distractor2_choice: Add a post-processing validation step that rejects sample sizes outside a plausible range and strips author names that appear in the references section.
distractor2_rationale: Post-hoc filters catch only the errors you can express as rules, and a fabricated sample size of 200 passes any plausibility check. Validation is a useful safety net but doesn't teach the model to read varied structures correctly, so precision stays low.
anti_pattern: Piling more prose rules onto an extraction prompt when the model misreads varied document structures. Prose is interpreted inconsistently; few-shot demonstrations across the actual structural variety (including an absent-field-returns-null example) are the reliable way to communicate the transformation.

stem: Your invoice extraction tool uses a JSON schema where vendor_tax_id and po_number are required string fields. In production, about 20% of invoices legitimately have no PO number, yet the extraction never returns an empty result — audit sampling reveals the model emits syntactically valid, entirely fabricated PO numbers for exactly those invoices. What schema-level fix addresses the root cause?
correct_choice: Make po_number (and any field that can be legitimately absent) optional/nullable in the schema, so the model can return null when the document doesn't contain the value.
correct_rationale: A required field forces the model to produce some value for every document; when the source lacks the information, the only way to satisfy the schema is fabrication. Designing possibly-absent fields as nullable removes that pressure — the exam guide names nullable field design specifically as the guard against fabricating values to satisfy required fields.
near_miss_choice: Keep the field required but add a prompt instruction: "Never fabricate a PO number; output an empty string if the invoice has no PO number."
near_miss_rationale: This pits a probabilistic prompt instruction against a hard schema constraint and overloads empty string as a sentinel the downstream system must special-case. The schema still declares the field required — the correct fix is to make the schema tell the truth about the data: the value is sometimes absent.
distractor1_choice: Add a validation-retry loop that rejects PO numbers not matching your PO format and re-requests extraction with the validation error attached.
distractor1_rationale: Fabricated PO numbers are often format-plausible and sail through validation; when one is rejected, retrying cannot succeed because the information doesn't exist in the source. Retry-with-feedback fixes format and structural errors, not missing information.
distractor2_choice: Lower the sampling temperature for extraction requests to reduce hallucination.
distractor2_rationale: The fabrication is driven by the schema constraint, not sampling randomness — at any temperature the model must fill a required field. Temperature tweaks don't change the fact that the schema demands a value the document doesn't contain.
anti_pattern: Declaring schema fields as required when the source documents may not contain the information. A required field forces the model to fabricate a plausible value to satisfy the schema; nullable/optional design lets it truthfully return null.

stem: You classify contract clauses into a fixed enum of eight clause types. Two problems have emerged in production: genuinely ambiguous clauses get forced into whichever category is closest (with no signal that the call was a coin flip), and new clause types your team encounters roughly monthly get silently shoehorned into existing categories, so nobody notices the taxonomy is stale. How should you evolve the schema?
correct_choice: Add an "unclear" enum value for ambiguous clauses, plus an "other" enum value paired with a free-text detail field; periodically review "other" entries to promote recurring patterns into first-class categories.
correct_rationale: The exam guide's schema-design guidance is exactly this pattern: "unclear" gives ambiguity an honest home instead of a forced guess, and "other" + detail keeps the enum's consistency for known categories while making novel types visible and reviewable — the detail field is the raw material for evolving the taxonomy.
near_miss_choice: Replace the enum with a free-text category field so the model can describe any clause type it encounters.
near_miss_rationale: Free text abandons the reason the enum exists: consistent, aggregatable labels. You'd get dozens of spellings and phrasings for the same category and lose the constrained-output guarantee for the 95% of clauses the taxonomy already covers.
distractor1_choice: Expand the enum to forty fine-grained clause types so every clause has a precise category.
distractor1_rationale: A larger label set increases decision complexity and misclassification between near-neighbors, and it still can't represent next month's genuinely novel type — the extensibility problem is structural, not a matter of enum size.
distractor2_choice: Keep the schema unchanged and instruct the model to always select the closest existing category so downstream aggregation stays stable.
distractor2_rationale: This institutionalizes the failure: ambiguous and novel clauses are silently mislabeled with full confidence, aggregation is stable but wrong, and taxonomy drift stays invisible. Stability of a miscategorized feed is not a virtue.

stem: Your contract extraction pipeline fails Pydantic validation on 18% of documents. Failure analysis shows two clusters: (a) format and structural errors — dates emitted as "Q3 FY24" instead of ISO 8601, line items that don't sum to the stated total; (b) contracts where termination_date is genuinely absent from the document. How should the retry strategy handle these clusters?
correct_choice: For cluster (a), retry with a follow-up request that includes the document, the failed extraction, and the specific validation errors; for cluster (b), don't retry — mark the field null (or route to review), since no retry can produce information absent from the source.
correct_rationale: Retry-with-error-feedback works when the model can self-correct: format mismatches and structural inconsistencies are fixable given the specific error. Retries are ineffective when the required information simply isn't in the document — the exam guide draws exactly this line, and burning retries on absent data adds cost and fabrication risk.
near_miss_choice: Retry both clusters up to three times, appending the validation errors to the prompt each time, and route documents that still fail to human review.
near_miss_rationale: Correct mechanics for cluster (a), but for cluster (b) every retry is guaranteed wasted work — and pressuring the model repeatedly for a value the document lacks invites fabrication. Distinguishing retryable from non-retryable failures before retrying is the point.
distractor1_choice: Retry both clusters with the same original prompt, since validation failures are usually transient sampling noise.
distractor1_rationale: Without the validation errors in the follow-up, the model has no signal about what to fix and tends to repeat the same output. And absent information is not noise — no number of identical retries manifests a termination date the contract doesn't contain.
distractor2_choice: Skip retries entirely and send all 18% of failed documents to human review.
distractor2_rationale: Format and structural errors are exactly what retry-with-error-feedback reliably fixes at machine cost. Sending the whole failure volume to humans forfeits that and swamps reviewer capacity with mechanically correctable cases.
anti_pattern: Retrying extraction when the required information is absent from the source document. Retry-with-error-feedback fixes format and structural errors; it cannot conjure missing information, and repeated pressure invites fabrication.

stem: Developers dismiss about 30% of your automated review's findings, but the dismissals are spread across free-text finding descriptions, so the team can't tell which review criteria generate the false positives. You want to systematically identify and fix the prompt criteria responsible. What change to the findings schema enables this?
correct_choice: Add a structured detected_pattern field recording which code construct or rule triggered each finding, so dismissed findings can be aggregated by pattern to reveal which criteria produce false positives.
correct_rationale: Systematic false-positive analysis needs a stable, aggregatable key linking each finding to the criterion that produced it. The exam guide names the detected_pattern field for exactly this feedback-loop design: group dismissals by pattern, find the criteria with the worst dismissal rates, and fix those prompts specifically.
near_miss_choice: Have the model output a confidence score with each finding and analyze whether dismissed findings cluster at low confidence.
near_miss_rationale: Self-reported confidence is poorly calibrated, and even where it correlates, it tells you findings were uncertain — not which criterion generated them. You still can't trace dismissals back to a specific prompt rule to fix.
distractor1_choice: Survey the development team quarterly about which kinds of findings they find least useful.
distractor1_rationale: Surveys are anecdotal, lag by months, and aren't linked to individual findings, so they can't quantify per-criterion false-positive rates. The dismissal events already happening are the ground truth — they just need a structured key to aggregate on.
distractor2_choice: Cap the review at ten findings per pull request so developers see fewer findings to dismiss.
distractor2_rationale: A cap hides volume without improving precision — the surviving ten findings have the same false-positive rate, real issues beyond the cap are silently dropped, and you gain no information about which criteria are misfiring.

stem: A compliance team runs two Claude workloads: (1) a nightly re-extraction of ~5,000 archived contracts feeding a weekly audit report, and (2) an onboarding check that must validate a new customer's contract within 30 seconds while they wait. API costs are under pressure, and last night's pilot batch run had 112 documents fail on context-length errors. What's the right architecture?
correct_choice: Use the Message Batches API for the nightly archive workload and keep the synchronous API for the onboarding check; for batch failures, identify failed documents by custom_id and resubmit only those, chunking the ones that exceeded context limits.
correct_rationale: The Batches API trades latency for a 50% cost saving with up to a 24-hour processing window and no latency SLA — ideal for the overnight, latency-tolerant archive job and unacceptable for a 30-second blocking check. custom_id correlates request/response pairs, so failure handling resubmits only the failed documents with fixes (chunking oversized ones) rather than rerunning all 5,000.
near_miss_choice: Move both workloads to the Batches API and poll aggressively for the onboarding batch, since batches often complete much faster than 24 hours.
near_miss_rationale: "Often faster" is not a guarantee — the Batches API has no latency SLA, so a customer-facing 30-second requirement can't be built on it no matter how aggressively you poll. Blocking workflows need the synchronous API.
distractor1_choice: Keep both workloads on the synchronous API but schedule the nightly job during off-peak hours to reduce cost.
distractor1_rationale: There is no off-peak discount on synchronous requests — time-of-day scheduling saves nothing. This forfeits the 50% batch discount on exactly the workload the Batches API is designed for: high-volume, latency-tolerant processing.
distractor2_choice: After a batch run with failures, resubmit the full 5,000-document batch so the results stay consistent within a single batch.
distractor2_rationale: custom_id exists precisely so you can correlate results and act per-document; there's no consistency requirement that results come from one batch. Resubmitting everything re-pays for ~4,900 successful extractions and re-fails the oversized documents unless they're also chunked.

stem: A framework-upgrade pull request touches 22 files. Your single-pass review (all files in one request) produces thorough feedback on the first several files and increasingly superficial notes on later ones, and it missed that a renamed API-client method broke three caller sites in other files. You have budget for a modest increase in request volume. How should you restructure the review?
correct_choice: Run a focused per-file pass for local issues in each file, then a separate integration pass that examines cross-file concerns like the renamed method's call sites and data flow between changed files.
correct_rationale: Attention dilutes when many files compete in one request — later files get superficial treatment, and cross-file reasoning gets crowded out entirely. Splitting into per-file local passes plus a dedicated cross-file integration pass gives every file consistent depth and makes inter-file data flow the explicit subject of its own pass, which is where the broken callers get caught.
near_miss_choice: Keep a single pass but reorder the files so the riskiest ones come first and get the deepest attention.
near_miss_rationale: Ordering decides which files get the superficial treatment, not whether any do — the dilution remains, just redistributed. And the missed breakage wasn't in any single file; it lives between files, which a single crowded pass still handles worst of all.
distractor1_choice: Raise max_tokens substantially so the model can write a longer, more detailed review of all 22 files in one pass.
distractor1_rationale: max_tokens caps output length; it doesn't improve how attention is allocated across a large input. You'd get a longer review with the same shallow coverage of later files and the same blind spot for cross-file interactions.
distractor2_choice: Run three independent full-PR review passes and report only findings that appear in at least two of the three.
distractor2_rationale: Every pass suffers the same attention dilution, so the intersection is biased toward early-file findings, and majority voting actively suppresses real issues caught intermittently. Cost triples without any pass being asked to examine cross-file data flow.
